name: Project GRILL FLAME
description: Project GRILL FLAME was a top-secret United States Army intelligence program that ran from 1978 to 1995, later renamed Project CENTER LANE. It involved the investigation of remote viewing, also known as psychic spying. Proponents claim that the program explored the potential for using individuals with alleged psychic abilities to gather intelligence on foreign targets, providing information inaccessible through conventional means. The project was initiated due to perceived successes of similar Soviet programs. Information gathering included attempts to view geographic locations, weapons systems, and even the thoughts of individuals. Participants were often selected based on claims of pre-existing psychic abilities. Mainstream science views remote viewing as a pseudoscience, lacking reproducible results and relying on subjective interpretations. Skeptics argue that reported 'successes' can be attributed to chance, vague or leading questions, or unintentional cues provided by researchers. Official documentation released under the Freedom of Information Act confirms the existence of the program, but its effectiveness remains a subject of debate. The program was ultimately terminated after a report concluded that it did not provide reliable intelligence data. Criticism also focuses on the lack of rigorous scientific methodology and the potential for bias in the selection and interpretation of results.
subtitle: US Army's Psychic Spying Program

claims.claim: Remote viewing can provide accurate intelligence information.
claims.evidence: Alleged successes in viewing specific locations and events, as reported by program participants and some researchers.
claims.criticism: Lack of reproducible results under controlled conditions; results attributable to chance, suggestion, or confirmation bias.

claims.claim: The Soviets had a successful psychic spying program, necessitating a US response.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports and intelligence assessments suggesting Soviet interest and investment in parapsychology.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence of Soviet program effectiveness; potential exaggeration of Soviet capabilities to justify US funding.

claims.claim: Project GRILL FLAME yielded valuable insights despite its eventual termination.
claims.evidence: Claims of successful target identification and information gathering, as documented in declassified reports.
claims.criticism: The final report concluded the program was not reliable and did not provide actionable intelligence. Any claimed successes are viewed as statistically insignificant.

keyFigures.name: Ingo Swann
keyFigures.role: Remote viewer and participant in the program

keyFigures.name: Edwin C. May
keyFigures.role: Scientist and director of the follow-up program, Stargate

keyFigures.name: Ray Hyman
keyFigures.role: Psychologist and prominent critic of parapsychology and remote viewing

timeline.event: Project GRILL FLAME renamed Project CENTER LANE
Project CENTER LANE terminated and replaced by Stargate Project
Independent assessment of Stargate Project deems remote viewing ineffective
